stem:
A grocery store chain operates 50 stores across a state. A manager randomly selected 10 of the 50 stores and recorded the number of customer complaints received at each store during one month. The results showed that, on average, each selected store received 24 complaints that month. The manager concluded that the average number of complaints per store across all 50 stores in the state was approximately 24 that month. Which of the following best evaluates this conclusion?

(A) The conclusion is not valid because 10 stores is not enough to represent 50 stores.
(B) The conclusion is valid because the 10 stores were randomly selected from all 50 stores in the chain, so the average from the sample can reasonably be applied to all 50 stores.
(C) The conclusion is not valid because the manager should have calculated the total number of complaints, not the average.
(D) The conclusion is valid because the manager personally selected the stores, ensuring an accurate sample.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The conclusion is not valid because 10 stores is not enough to represent 50 stores.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The conclusion is valid because the 10 stores were randomly selected from all 50 stores in the chain, so the average from the sample can reasonably be applied to all 50 stores.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The conclusion is not valid because the manager should have calculated the total number of complaints, not the average.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The conclusion is valid because the manager personally selected the stores, ensuring an accurate sample.

explanation:
Step 1: Identify the claim. The manager claims the average number of complaints across all 50 stores is approximately 24.

Step 2: Evaluate the sampling method. The 10 stores were randomly selected from all 50 stores. Random selection gives each store an equal chance of being included, which means the sample is representative of the population of all 50 stores.

Step 3: Assess the sample size. A sample of 10 out of 50 stores represents 20% of the population. With random selection, this is a reasonable sample from which to estimate the population average.

Step 4: Evaluate the inference. Because the sample was randomly selected from the population of interest (all 50 stores), using the sample average of 24 complaints to estimate the population average is a valid inference.

Answer B correctly identifies random selection as the key condition that makes the inference valid.

distractorRationale.A: Incorrect. A sample of 10 out of 50 is 20% of the population. When randomly selected, this is a sufficient proportion to support an inference. Rejecting the conclusion based only on the count of 10 ignores the importance of random selection.
distractorRationale.B: Correct. Random selection from the full population of 50 stores allows the sample average to be used as a reasonable estimate of the population average.
distractorRationale.C: Incorrect. Computing an average number of complaints per store is an appropriate and common way to describe and compare data. There is no requirement to use totals instead of averages for a valid inference.
distractorRationale.D: Incorrect. The conclusion is valid, but for the wrong reason. The manager personally choosing which stores to include would be a judgment sample, not a random sample — which could introduce bias. The reason the conclusion is valid is that the stores were randomly selected, not personally chosen.
